era_context: The Abbasid Caliphate under al-Ma'mun is in its final years; the caliph is aging. The Carolingian Empire under Louis the Pious is beginning to fragment as his sons contest the succession. The Tang Dynasty continues its gradual decline. Viking raids on Western Europe are beginning to intensify. In the Islamic world, the Mu'tazilite theological movement is at its height of influence.

events.title: Abbasid Caliph al-Ma'mun continues military campaigns and administrative consolidation
events.description: Al-Ma'mun, now in his late 50s, continues to consolidate Abbasid power through military campaigns and administrative reforms. His reign has been marked by the patronage of Mu'tazilite theology, the establishment of the House of Wisdom in Baghdad, and military campaigns against the Byzantine Empire and internal rivals. In 831, al-Ma'mun is still actively governing, though his health is beginning to decline. His reign will end with his death in 833.

sources.name: al-Tabari, History of the Prophets and Kings, 9th–10th century

events.certainty_note: Al-Ma'mun's continued governance in 831 is attested in Islamic historiography, but specific events and dates may be conflated or misdated in later compilation.

events.title: Carolingian succession crisis: Louis the Pious faces challenges from his sons; civil war begins
events.description: The aging Carolingian emperor Louis the Pious faces increasing challenges to his authority from his sons, particularly Lothair I, who contests the division of the empire. The year 831 marks the beginning of the serious civil conflict that will characterise the 830s. Louis attempts to maintain control, but his authority is increasingly challenged by his sons' ambitions and the support they receive from various Frankish nobles and bishops.
key_figures: Louis the German (future king of East Francia)
Charles the Bald (future king of West Francia)

sources.name: Astronomer, Life of Louis the Pious, 9th century

events.certainty_note: The beginning of the Carolingian succession crisis in 831 is recorded in Frankish chronicles. The date is secure.
cross_references: Precedes Battle of Fontenoy-en-Puisaye (833) and Treaty of Verdun (843)

events.title: Viking raids on English monasteries; Norse activity intensifies in North Sea
events.description: Viking raids on English monasteries and coastal settlements intensify in 831. These raids are part of the broader pattern of Norse expansion into Western Europe during the 830s. The targeting of monasteries is partly due to their wealth and relative lack of fortification. The exact targets and scale of individual raids in 831 are not always clearly documented, but the pattern of escalating Norse activity is evident.
events.certainty_note: Viking activity in 831 is attested in the Anglo-Saxon Chronicle, but specific raids may be conflated or misdated in later compilation.
cross_references: Part of broader Viking Age expansion in 9th century

events.title: Tang Dynasty: Wenzong's reign continues; eunuch-literati factional conflict
events.description: Emperor Wenzong of Tang (r. 827–840) continues to face factional conflict between eunuchs and scholar-officials (literati) within the imperial court. The year 831 sees continued political instability as these factions compete for influence over imperial policy. This internal conflict contributes to the broader decline of Tang central authority.

sources.name: Zizhi Tongjian (Comprehensive Mirror for Aid in Government), compiled by Sima Guang, 11th century
Old Book of Tang (Jiu Tangshu), 10th century

events.certainty_note: The factional conflicts of Wenzong's reign are well-documented in later Chinese historiography, but precise dating of individual episodes to 831 is conventional.

disconfirming_evidence: The exact beginning of the Carolingian succession crisis is sometimes placed in 830 rather than 831, depending on the source and interpretation. Some scholars argue that the crisis was more gradual than the dramatic narrative of the chronicles suggests. The significance of specific Viking raids in 831 may be overstated in later sources.
historiographic_note: The year 831 is moderately well-documented in Frankish and Islamic sources, though both are filtered through later compilation. The Frankish sources on the civil war are relatively abundant but often reflect the perspective of the victorious sons. Islamic sources on al-Ma'mun are generally reliable but written decades or centuries after the events. Chinese sources on the Tang Dynasty are abundant but reflect the perspective of the literati class. Sub-Saharan Africa, Southeast Asia, and the Americas are absent from contemporary written records.

graph_edges.note: Carolingian succession crisis of 831 leads to Battle of Fontenoy-en-Puisaye in 833
Escalating Viking raids of 831 are part of broader pattern leading to intensified Norse activity in 835
Al-Ma'mun's continued governance in 831 precedes his death and succession of al-Mu'tasim in 833
